---
title: "GMS: Building the Core Services"
subtitle: "Grading Management System"
author: "Yuvraj Rathod"
date: "2026-07-12"
description: "An inside look at building the core microservices of GMS incrementally, implementing communication patterns, testing APIs, and separating business logic."
categories: [Software Engineering, Open Source]
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    link-external-newwindow: true
---

With the design and folder blueprint established in the [previous posts](../gms_3/index.qmd), the project moved from planning to implementation. [Blog 1](../gms_1/index.qmd) explained the problem and motivation, [Blog 2](../gms_2/index.qmd) laid out the system design and data ownership, and [Blog 3](../gms_3/index.qmd) defined the tech stack and monorepo layout. Here I describe how the core backend services were built in practice, why I ordered work the way I did, and the practical lessons learned while keeping the codebase clean and maintainable.

## Why build services incrementally

I built services one at a time to reduce feedback loops and limit blast radius. The approach is simple:

- Implement a small, testable slice of functionality
- Wire it into the gateway and auth flows
- Exercise it with unit and integration tests
- Then iterate or move to the next service

### Start with gateway and auth: the project front door

The `gateway` and `auth_user` service are the foundation. The `gateway` acts as a lightweight router and a place to implement cross-cutting concerns (CORS, request logging, rate limit, simple auth checks). `auth_user` owns identity and basic user metadata. I focused on these first because:

- Other services depend on authenticated requests and user IDs
- Consistent, testable auth behavior reduces later surprises
- Gateway allows local end-to-end testing without coupling services tightly

### Courses: core domain and reference data

The `courses` service stores the canonical course structure and role mappings. Because almost every other service references a course or a course role, it acts as a reference service. I made these choices when implementing it:

- Keep the course object compact (ID, code, title, term, metadata)
- Expose role endpoints (`/courses/{id}/roles`) used by `gateway`, `marks`, and `policy`
- Validate input strictly with **Pydantic** models to avoid schema drift

### Marks: focused on data correctness

The `marks` service is the primary write surface for assessment entries. Its responsibilities:

- Accept bulk uploads and single-entry updates
- Enforce constraints (max marks, assessment visibility)
- Publish events when marks are published (**RabbitMQ**)

### Policy: encapsulate grading rules

Grading rules are complex and change frequently. I separated the policy logic into its own service so rules could evolve independently from marks storage. The `policy` service:

- Stores course-level grading policies and assessment categories
- Computes student totals according to configured rules (best-N, custom weights, cumulative)
- Exposes an API for on-demand computation and also consumes mark-published events to precompute totals

### Analytics: reporting and background processing

Analytics reads data from service-owned stores (via safe read APIs or replicated views) and builds derived data: mean, median, frequencies, and distribution charts. Key points:

- Analytics processing is async - triggered by messages (**RabbitMQ**) when marks are published
- Keep analytics models denormalized for fast reads
- Design retention and rollup strategies early to avoid long-term storage bloat

## Communication patterns and decoupling

Following Blog 2, services communicate with minimal coupling. I used two primary patterns:

- Synchronous HTTP for direct queries and transactional operations (e.g., course membership checks)
- Asynchronous messaging (**RabbitMQ**) for events that can be processed later (e.g., marks published → analytics, policy recompute)

## Testing strategy while building

Tests were an essential safety net at every step. My testing approach:

- Unit tests for service logic (business rules, validators)
- Small integration tests for route → service interaction using `TestClient`
- Contract checks between services (example: `courses` API returns fields that `marks` expects)
- End-to-end smoke test that runs `gateway` + a minimal set of services together
- Use **Postman** for manual API endpoint testing and iterative debugging

## Deployment

- Containerize each service (`Dockerfile` in each service folder)
- Provide small, reproducible Kubernetes manifests in `manifests/` for each service
- Use a simple deployment script for local clusters and a separate manifest set for production

The final blog describes how these choices came together during the actual deployment and testing with students.

## Practical lessons and tradeoffs

- Start small and keep APIs stable - breaking changes are costly once multiple services depend on them
- Separate rules (`policy`) from data (`marks`) to make policy experimentation safer
- Prefer event-driven updates for heavy work like analytics
- Invest in tests and observability early to reduce debugging time later

## Closing thoughts

This phase was where the design from [Blog 1](../gms_1/index.qmd)–[3](../gms_3/index.qmd) turned into running services. Implementing services one by one, enforcing clear contracts, and keeping communication patterns simple helped the project scale from a prototype into a working platform. The next blog walks through deployment, Kubernetes manifests, and the beta test with over 330 students.
